Medaram Jatara

[Telugu History](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/telugu-history/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Medaram Jatara

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Jan 29, 2026

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mV5C!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F80416598-b516-4a4f-9db9-2b6a4333ead6_622x373.jpeg)

![This image has an empty alt attribute; its file name is med.jpg](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mV5C!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F80416598-b516-4a4f-9db9-2b6a4333ead6_622x373.jpeg "This image has an empty alt attribute; its file name is med.jpg")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Medaram is a tiny hamlet located in the Tadwai mandal of Mulugu district, in the newly formed Telangana state. Located around 110 km from the district headquarters, the hamlet is located midst thick forests, hills and valleys. Every 2 years during the month of Magha, this hamlet is the occasion for a unique tribal festival of it’s kind. In a sense, the deities worshiped here are not sages, they are people who sacrificed their life for a cause, for their own people.

Samakka, Sarakka Jatra that occurs here every year celebrates the martyrdom of two tribal woman, in their battle against the mighty Kakatiya Empire. The two women, led the tribals against none other than the great Kakatiya ruler, Prataparudra. In a sense this Jatra celebrates the power of Shakti, of womanhood, and also in a way, the importance given to women in tribal culture.

**Back Story**

The Kakatiyas were one of the great kingdoms in the South, ruling from Warangal( then Orugallu) as their capital. One of the mightiest empires, then a great civilization, that ruled over Warangal and surrounding areas. Medaram was ruled by Pagidida Raju, a chieftain owing allegiance to the Kakatiyas. He was the maternal nephew of Meda Raju, the ruler of Enugandla( present day Karimnagar). Samakka, was the wife of Pagidida Raju, it’s believed she was found in the forest by Meda Raju, who then gave her in marriage to his nephew. Their children were Saralamma, Nagulamma, Jampanna. The eldest among them Saralamma, was married to Govindarajulu. The Medaram area, faced a terrible drought for three years, most of the tribals were on the verge of starvation. With the drought devastating their crops, the tribals were in no position to pay taxes to the Kakatiya emperor. However to the rulers, the request of the tribals to waive the taxes, sounded like insubordination, and treason to the king. And Pratapa Rudra sent the army to forcibly collect the taxes from the tribals. It was an unequal battle, the impoverished tribals against the might of the Kakatiyas.

The tribals however did not lose heart, and they fought back against the Kakatiyas. The battle was fought at Sampangi Vagu( basically a stream), on the boundaries of Medaram. Pagidida Raju, his daughters Nagulamma, Saralamma and his son in law Govindaraju, all died in the battle against the Kakatiyas. Unable to bear the humiliation, his son Jampanna, committed suicide by drowning himself in the Sampangi Vagu. It is believed that the reddish color of this stream, was his blood, and it has been renamed as Jampanna Vagu in his honor.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ebIr!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fec319eeb-acca-4177-a053-055cf8e433e2_537x270.jpeg)

![This image has an empty alt attribute; its file name is med1.jpg](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!ebIr!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fec319eeb-acca-4177-a053-055cf8e433e2_537x270.jpeg "This image has an empty alt attribute; its file name is med1.jpg")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

A distraught Samakka, broke down on hearing about the tragic news. She however refused to be cowed down, and took to arms. She fell on the Kakatiya forces, with fury, and attacked them. So severe was her assault, that even the battle hardened Kakatiya forces, had to scatter, and some even fled from the battlefield. It would have been the ultimate humiliation for the mighty Kakatiya empire, to be defeated by a rag tag tribal army. And so they began to influence other tribal soldiers with money and force. The tactics worked, and Samakka was stabbed in the back by one of her own people. A bleeding Samakka, managed to go till Chilakalagutla to the North east of Medaram, and disappeared somewhere there. Only a small kumkum case, pointed to the presence of Samakka there, which the tribals took as a symbol. Just then a divine voice spoke saying

> *“Winning a kingdom through treachery is not bravery, every one born
> on this land should win a kingdom by bravery, not deceit. Build two
> logs here, and hold a Jatra every two years, I shall come and make
> devotees wishes come true”.*

The tribals heeded what they believed was the voice of Samakka herself. In due course of time, Pratapa Rudra, introspected, and felt guilty about what he did. He waived the taxes for the tribal people, owing to the drought. He made offerings to Samakka, and gave orders that the Jatara should be held every two years there.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mdcx!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe162bbf2-2f8b-457b-9be4-6531a3bbbe1d_500x339.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mdcx!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe162bbf2-2f8b-457b-9be4-6531a3bbbe1d_500x339.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

**The Festival**

Medaram where the Jatra is observed every two years, is located close to the Chattisgarh border, and surrounded by Khammam, Karimnagar districts. This was declared as an official festival by the Government of Telangana. Lakhs of devotees come to this Jatra, not just from Telangana, Andhra Pradesh, but also tribals from Chattisgarh, Odisha, Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, Jharkhand too. Chenchus, Gonds, Lambadis, Bhils, all gather at one place, making this one of the largest tribal festivals ever in India.

All the devotees visiting this place, first take bath in the Jampanna Vagu, and from then proceed to the darshan of Samakka, Sarakka. The log bearing likeness to Samakka, is placed on a platform, to the sounds of chants by devotees, and shots fired in the air by the top police officers of Warangal district. Since tribals don’t believe in idols or even temples, the bamboo logs and the kumkum case are the only representation. Tribals generally indulge in nature worship, and here the two bamboo logs, represent Samakka, Sarakka. The log representing Saralammma( or Sarakka) is bought from the village of Kanepalli, around 3 Km from Medaram.

The representation of Sarakka is then placed on the dias, in the presence of 6 priests, many devotees, prostrate seeking her blessings. The entire journey from Kanepalli to Medaram takes 6 hours, by foot, with crowds thronging the route, seeking her blessings.

Another group starts from Punagonda village, with the log idol of Pagiddi Raju, while her husband Govinda Raju is bought from Kondai village in Eturunagaram mandal. And on the same day, the log idols of Sarakka, her husband Govinda Raju and father Pagiddi Raju are placed on the mounted platform. For 4 days the entire venue reverberates with the chants of “Samakka Ko, Saralamma Ko”.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PV2y!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd8bc16d4-d3fd-40cb-add6-a551bc0d54e0_252x200.jpeg)

![This image has an empty alt attribute; its file name is sam.jpg](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PV2y!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd8bc16d4-d3fd-40cb-add6-a551bc0d54e0_252x200.jpeg "This image has an empty alt attribute; its file name is sam.jpg")

Many devotees pledge to donate gold, to Samakka and Sarakka. The gold however here is not the yellow metal, for the tribals, it is the red gold, aka jaggery. The sweet jaggery is the most important possession for the tribals. It is believed that Samakka herself came in a dream to a devotee and said that jaggery offering is equivalent to gold for her. For most of the poor tribals, not in a position to own, gold, this humble jaggery offering is their equivalent. It also underscores the importance of devotion, when offering, the sweetness in jaggery, is a symbol of devotion and love.

The devotees also offer puffed rice, which they believe is dear to Samakka. The women devotees, put the puffed rice in a cloth, and carry it to Samakka. Along with the puffed rice, the cloth also has Haldi( turmeric), kumkum, 2 dry coconut shells,2 blouse pieces,dates and two betel nuts, and it’s tied to the waist of these women. There is also the tradition of releasing cocks during the Jatra, to please the deity. Men also dress up as women, to please the deity during the Jatra. The Jatra is auspicious to many tribals, giving the hair, getting married, all take place here. It is believed that any important work, that is started at this time, will eventually be successful

After 4 days, the deities are returned back to the forest. This Jatra has seen a massive increase in crowds, in recent times. Not just tribals, many non tribals too have been vistign the Jatra in large numbers, undertaking part in the festivities. But this has been primarily a festival of and for the tribals. It reflects the traditional tribal rituals and modes of worship.

The Chikudukayala Panduga( Festival of beans) during January is connected to this, when devotees offer the wild grass, brooms and beans to the Vana Devatas( tribal goddesses). Meat obtained from hunting is given to the deities during Mondemerige Panduga, and new clothes are tied to trees during the Vippa Poola Panduga. All these rituals and celebrations, tie in with the tribal way of life, of living in harmony with nature and nature worship.
